title: Uterine cancer in does and the case for spaying
subtitle: Understanding the risk in older unspayed does and what to do about it.
excerpt: Uterine adenocarcinoma is common in unspayed female rabbits, with some estimates suggesting over 80% of intact does over five years old develop it. For a doe kept as a pet or a long-lived breeding animal, spaying before two years of age removes the risk entirely. Here's what the disease looks like, when spaying makes sense, and what it involves.
sourceNotes: British Rabbit Council welfare guidelines. University of Wisconsin Extension, Rabbit Health and Husbandry. RSPCA rabbit health and welfare guidance.

glossaryTerms.definition: A malignant tumour of the uterine lining, the most common cancer in unspayed female rabbits; begins as a benign hyperplasia and becomes aggressive over time.

content.text: Unspayed does that are not regularly bred are at high risk of

as they age. A doe that bred regularly and then stopped is at higher risk than one that bred continuously throughout her productive years. The disease is almost always preventable by spaying before two years of age. For meat does that cycle through a breeding programme and are retired at three to four years, the risk profile is lower but still real.
Signs to watch for in older does
Blood-tinged discharge from the vulva, the earliest sign
Lumpy masses palpable in the lower abdomen
Lethargy and reduced interest in food in the later stages

Spay a pet doe at around four to six months, before her first breeding season and while she is young and healthy. This is a routine procedure for a vet experienced with rabbits. Spaying an older doe with early tumours is more involved and carries more risk.

For meat rabbits retired from breeding: the practical question is whether the remaining productive life justifies the spay cost and anaesthetic risk. A doe being retired at two years and kept for two more years as a colony companion is a reasonable candidate. A doe retiring at four years is probably past the point where spaying is the right calculation.

Rabbit spaying removes both ovaries and the uterus. Rabbits need different anaesthetic management from cats and dogs. A vet experienced with rabbits won't fast the animal for more than two hours. They'll use an inhalant gas rather than injectable drugs. Ask whether the vet sees rabbits regularly before booking. Ask specifically whether the vet operates on rabbits regularly, this is not a procedure to take to a dog-and-cat practice without specialist rabbit experience.

A doe recovering from spaying needs to eat within four to six hours of leaving anaesthesia to keep her gut moving. Offer hay, fresh greens, and a favourite food immediately on return. Monitor for gut stasis, a doe that stops eating and moving post-surgery needs vet attention within 12 hours.
